role_summary: San Diego CORE co-founder and chapter chairman in the early 1960s; earned his San Diego State University degree in 1959 and returned in 1971 as the university's first African American administrator; namesake of SDSU's Harold K. Brown Civil Rights and African American Experience Collection.
ai_reading: Brown's semantic neighbors in this corpus cross ethnic, regional, and disciplinary categories more than is typical here. Ranked by shared-passage count his top five are NAACP Legal Defense Fund lawyer Robert L. Carter (entry 108, count 181), Chicano civil rights organizer Mateo Camarillo (entry 86, count 178), athlete Bill Russell (entry 8, count 110), Robert Brown (entry 106, count 106), and West Coast author Mildred Pitts Walter (entry 91, count 103); the highest single cosine score on the list, 0.83, runs to Roberta Alexander (entry 110), a San Diego Black Panther organizer. The embedding therefore reads his San Diego CORE leadership as a cross-coalition West Coast voice, a pattern the cultural record's local-Black-organizer label does not foreground; most clusters in this corpus concentrate inside one faction or one geography. On the Tactical Pragmatism versus Strategic Vision axis his normalized position is -0.51, placing him toward Strategic Vision, consistent with a chapter-founder and Western Region chairman whose testimony frames the sit-ins, mail-ins, the penny coin-in, and repeated arrests as instruments of a sustained, multi-year campaign to build employment-discrimination and fair-housing pressure rather than as one-off actions.
biographical_paragraph: Harold K. Brown, known as Hal, was born in 1934 in York, Pennsylvania, and moved to San Diego in 1953 on a basketball recruitment to San Diego State, an arrival interrupted by two years of Army service before he completed his degree there in 1959 [src: 1, 2]. Around 1960 to 1961 he joined a new San Diego chapter of the Congress of Racial Equality and was elected its chairman, later chairing CORE's Western Region [src: 2, 3]. The chapter made employment discrimination its top priority, running marches, sit-ins, mail-ins, and a penny coin-in against the San Diego Zoo, Montgomery Ward, San Diego Gas & Electric, and Bank of America; Brown served jail time, on weekends so he could keep teaching, for the Bank of America and San Diego Gas & Electric demonstrations [src: 3]. He also worked the El Cajon Valley Open Housing Committee against the racially restrictive covenants that confined Black residents to Logan Heights [src: 3]. After a Peace Corps year in Lesotho, he returned to San Diego State in 1971 as the university's first African American administrator, Assistant to the Vice President for Academic Affairs, and in 1972 established its Afro-American Studies program, now the Department of Africana Studies [src: 2, 3]. He retired from SDSU in 2004, received an honorary doctorate from the university in 2017, and died on his ninety-second birthday, May 6, 2026 [src: 2]. His Library of Congress oral history is entry 55 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) [src: 1].

interview_snippets.speaker: Harold K. Brown
interview_snippets.lead_in: Asked to begin with his childhood, Brown reframed the whole interview as a single question about origins:
interview_snippets.quote: I like to start by saying that, you know, I'm trying to answer the question is what breeds a civil rights activist or a civil rights militant as we were called in the 60s.

interview_snippets.speaker: Harold K. Brown
interview_snippets.lead_in: On how he entered San Diego CORE leadership after waiting out his first year of teaching:
interview_snippets.quote: And then I decided, OK, I'll go and then I became the chairman of the San Diego CORE.

interview_snippets.speaker: Harold K. Brown
interview_snippets.lead_in: Brown on what the San Diego chapter chose to fight first, a focus that set West Coast CORE apart from the southern voter-registration campaigns:
interview_snippets.quote: And we found that to be serious, but we found employment discrimination to be really our top priority.

interview_snippets.speaker: Harold K. Brown
interview_snippets.lead_in: On the campaigns that put him in jail:
interview_snippets.quote: And our biggest project was against the Bank of America, which that's where I received my jail sentences from the Bank of America demonstrations and also from the San Diego Gas and Electric demonstrations.

interview_snippets.speaker: Harold K. Brown
interview_snippets.lead_in: Brown describes the coin-in, a teller-window tactic the San Diego chapter used to pressure the banks:
interview_snippets.quote: The coin in was we would stand in line at the Tellers window. We would have a lot of pennies with us and we would either ask for pennies.

interview_snippets.speaker: Harold K. Brown
interview_snippets.lead_in: Looking back, Brown argued the movement had left one front unworked, and located economics outside the reach of its best-known leader:
interview_snippets.quote: I mean Martin Luther King has great as he was, you know, anything about economics and business and all that stuff. It wasn't his area. He was a theology.

movement_context: The Congress of Racial Equality (CORE) that Brown organized for in San Diego was founded in 1942 in Chicago by James Farmer, George Houser, Bernice Fisher, and a small Fellowship of Reconciliation cohort committed to applying Gandhian nonviolent direct action to U.S. racial segregation; the chapter-federated structure CORE adopted from its founding meant that a city-level chairman like Brown was both the chapter's institutional anchor and the link between local protest tactics and the national CORE leadership Farmer ran from New York [src: 4, 5]. The 1960-1964 period in which Brown's San Diego chapter formed was the documented peak of CORE chapter expansion across the country: August Meier and Elliott Rudwick's institutional history records that CORE's chapter count and direct-action activity rose sharply after the Greensboro sit-ins of February 1960 and the Farmer-organized 1961 Freedom Rides put CORE on the national civil rights stage alongside SNCC and SCLC [src: 4]. West Coast CORE chapters in this period concentrated their organizing on issues distinct from the southern movement's voter-registration focus: employment discrimination at major retailers, banks, and utilities, fair-housing campaigns against the racially restrictive covenants and lending practices that segregated California neighborhoods, and police-misconduct accountability; Mark Brilliant's institutional history of California civil rights organizing documents the specific arenas West Coast CORE chapters worked in during this period and the cross-racial coalition character that distinguished California campaigns from southern ones [src: 6]. Brown's own testimony names the targets of the San Diego chapter, Bank of America and San Diego Gas & Electric, with the San Diego Zoo and Montgomery Ward added in the finding aid for his papers, alongside his work on the El Cajon Valley Open Housing Committee against restrictive covenants [src: 3]. His cross-coalition embedding signature with NAACP LDF, Chicano civil rights, and athlete-activist neighbors, surfaced in the AI's reading above, is structurally consistent with the cross-racial coalition pattern Brilliant documents for California's civil rights organizations [src: 6].
legacy_and_reception: The institutional record of Brown's legacy concentrates at San Diego State University, where he earned his degree in 1959, returned in 1971 as the campus's first African American administrator, established the Afro-American Studies program in 1972 (now the Department of Africana Studies), retired in 2004, and received an honorary doctorate in 2017 [src: 2]. His papers are held by the SDSU Library Special Collections and University Archives as the Harold K. Brown Papers (1956-2000, bulk 1963-1965 and the 1990s); the 4.59-linear-foot collection holds correspondence, reports, meeting minutes, slides, reel-to-reel recordings of the KSDO Radio program Viewpoint, photographs, and newspaper clippings documenting CORE's actions against the employment practices of the San Diego Zoo, San Diego Gas & Electric, Montgomery Ward, and Bank of America [src: 3]. The Library of Congress's inclusion of his interview as entry 55 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) places his testimony in the same federally curated oral-history record that documents the principal organizers of the movement nationally [src: 1]; the Civil Rights History Project was created by 2009 federal legislation (Public Law 111-19, the Civil Rights History Project Act) directing the Library of Congress and the Smithsonian's National Museum of African American History and Culture to record and preserve oral histories of movement participants [src: 7]. Independent scholarly biographical treatment of Brown's San Diego organizing is sparse in the published institutional-history literature outside the SDSU collection's finding aid and the LoC interview record; the catalog will expand this section as additional citable sources surface.

gallery.alt: James L. Farmer Jr. mugshot, Jackson, Mississippi police department, May 24, 1961
gallery.caption: James Farmer, CORE national director and co-founder, in his Jackson, Mississippi police mugshot after his arrest as a Freedom Rider on May 24, 1961. CORE's national chapter structure that Farmer led from New York was the network within which Brown organized San Diego CORE during the 1960-1964 chapter-expansion period.

gallery.alt: Attorney General Robert F. Kennedy addressing a CORE rally outside the Justice Department, June 14, 1963
gallery.caption: Attorney General Robert F. Kennedy speaking to a Congress of Racial Equality rally outside the U.S. Department of Justice on June 14, 1963. The photograph documents the period when CORE's national prominence, built through Farmer's leadership and the 1961 Freedom Rides, drew federal-executive attention; Brown's San Diego chapter was organizing during the same window.
gallery.repository: Wikimedia Commons (U.S. News and World Report collection, Library of Congress)

gallery.alt: Hepner Hall, San Diego State University, June 2013
gallery.caption: Hepner Hall, the historic Mission Revival administration building on the San Diego State University campus. SDSU is the institution from which Brown graduated in 1959 and to which he returned in 1971 as its first African American administrator, establishing the Afro-American Studies program in 1972; the Harold K. Brown Civil Rights and African American Experience Collection is held by the university library.

sources.title: Harold K. Brown oral history interview conducted by David P. Cline in San Diego, California, 2016 June 28

sources.title: SDSU Administrator Harold (Hal) K. Brown, 1934-2026

sources.title: Harold K. Brown Papers, 1956-2000, bulk 1963-1965, 1990s (finding aid)

sources.publisher: Arbor House, 1985; CORE national director's memoir of the chapter-federated organizing structure within which local CORE chairmen like Brown operated
